input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator**, designed to regulate temperature within an insulated container. The system operates in the **thermal control domain** and comprises the following key components:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to minimize external environmental influences.
  - **Heating Element**: A **heatbed** responsible for generating and maintaining internal heat.
  - **Air Circulation**: A **fan** to distribute heat uniformly inside the box.
  - **Sensing Infrastructure**: Two **temperature sensors** for monitoring internal temperature (critical for control) and one **external temperature sensor** for environmental context.
  - **Control Unit**: A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the embedded controller for real-time temperature regulation.
  The system is **suitable for digital twinning** due to its **dynamic, closed-loop control nature**, reliance on real-time sensor data, and need for predictive maintenance, fault detection, and adaptive optimization under varying environmental conditions (e.g., external temperature fluctuations). The **hardware specifications** are publicly documented in the repository [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, categorized by functional capabilities:
  1. **Real-Time Monitoring**:
     - **Safety Monitoring**: Uses **data-driven algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors) to continuously verify system performance against predefined safety thresholds (e.g., temperature limits).
     - **State Estimation**: Employs **state estimators** to reconstruct the internal temperature dynamics from sensor data, accounting for uncertainties in the physical system.
  2. **Predictive Simulation & Optimization**:
     - **Behavioral Prediction**: Executes **simulations** (e.g., differential equation-based models) to replicate and forecast the **thermal dynamics** of the incubator under current/perturbed operating conditions (e.g., external temperature changes, sensor failures).
     - **Parameter Calibration**: Implements **calibration algorithms** to iteratively refine model parameters (e.g., thermal conductivity, heat transfer coefficients) using real-time sensor data, ensuring alignment between the DT and PT.
     - **Optimization**: Applies **optimization techniques** (e.g., supervisory controllers) to adjust control policies (e.g., heatbed power, fan speed) for **energy efficiency** or **temperature stability**.
  3. **Visualization & Decision Support**:
     - **Advanced Visualizations**: Enables **real-time dashboards** or 3D representations (e.g., temperature gradients, fault locations) to aid engineers in diagnosing issues or validating simulations.
     - **Reconfigurability**: Supports **dynamic reconfiguration** of control strategies (e.g., switching between PID and model-predictive control) to adapt to **changing environmental conditions** (e.g., external temperature spikes).
  4. **Control & Actuation**:
     - **Supervisory Control**: Integrates with the **Raspberry Pi controller** to override or suggest corrective actions (e.g., emergency cooling) when deviations exceed thresholds.
     - **Closed-Loop Validation**: Uses **monitored data** to validate the effectiveness of control algorithms in real-time, enabling iterative improvements.
  **Target Users**:
     - **Engineers**: For system analysis, fault diagnosis, and optimization.
     - **Operators**: For real-time decision-making (e.g., adjusting setpoints).
     - **Maintenance Teams**: For predictive maintenance scheduling based on simulated wear-and-tear scenarios.
  The DT leverages **data-driven models** (e.g., differential equations) and **algorithm-driven services** (e.g., fault detection, state estimation) to bridge the gap between the **cyber (DT) and physical (PT) domains**, as depicted in the schematic overview (Fig. 1).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the incubator’s **Digital Twin** relies on the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles:
  1. **Modeling & Simulation**:
     - **Differential Equation-Based Models**: Used to represent **thermal dynamics** (e.g., heat transfer, convection) of the incubator, as referenced in [3, Section 5]. These models are **calibrated** using real-time sensor data to improve accuracy.
     - **Simulation Engines**: Likely employs **numerical solvers** (e.g., Runge-Kutta methods) for real-time or offline simulation of the PT’s behavior under varying conditions.
  2. **Data Acquisition & Processing**:
     - **Sensor Data Collection**: Real-time temperature data from **two internal sensors** and **one external sensor**, sampled at unspecified frequencies (assumed high-resolution for control purposes).
     - **Data Storage**: Implicit reliance on a **time-series database** or **logging system** to store sensor data for historical analysis and model calibration.
  3. **Control & Algorithms**:
     - **Embedded Controller**: **Raspberry Pi** running **real-time control algorithms** (e.g., PID, model-predictive control) interfaced with the DT for supervisory decisions.
     - **Calibration Algorithms**: Techniques such as **least-squares optimization** or **Bayesian inference** to adjust model parameters (e.g., thermal resistance) based on sensor-PT discrepancies.
     - **Fault Detection**: **Anomaly detection algorithms** (e.g., statistical process control, machine learning classifiers) to identify deviations in sensor data or system behavior.
     - **State Estimators**: **Kalman Filters** or **Particle Filters** for reconstructing the internal temperature state from noisy sensor measurements.
  4. **Software & Development Tools**:
     - **Programming Frameworks**: Likely uses **Python** (for algorithm development) and **C/C++** (for embedded control on the Raspberry Pi), given the repository’s context.
     - **Version Control**: **GitHub** ([https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) hosts the **hardware/software specifications**, including **up-to-date configurations** of the CPS and DT.
     - **Simulation Platforms**: Potential use of **MATLAB/Simulink**, **Modelica**, or **custom Python-based simulators** for model development and testing.
  5. **Visualization & Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**:
     - **Real-Time Dashboards**: Tools like **Grafana**, **LabVIEW**, or **custom web interfaces** to display temperature profiles, fault alerts, and simulation results.
     - **3D Modeling**: **CAD tools** (e.g., SolidWorks, Blender) for creating **geometric representations** of the incubator, integrated with thermal simulation data.
  6. **Communication Protocols**:
     - **Sensor-Controller Interface**: Likely uses **I2C**, **SPI**, or **serial communication** for Raspberry Pi-sensor interactions.
     - **DT-PT Interface**: **MQTT** or **OPC UA** for lightweight, real-time data exchange between the DT and PT sensors/controllers.
  **Key Enablers**:
     - **Cyber-Physical Integration**: Seamless coupling of **physical sensors**, **embedded controllers**, and **cyber models** to enable **closed-loop digital twinning**.
     - **Calibration Loop**: Continuous **model-PT synchronization** via **data-driven parameter updates**, ensuring predictive accuracy.
     - **Reconfigurability**: Modular design allows **swapping algorithms** (e.g., replacing PID with adaptive control) without hardware changes.
  The **Incubator Digital Twin** exemplifies a **minimal yet representative CPS-DT system**, leveraging **open-source tools**, **real-time control**, and **data-driven calibration** to achieve **safety, predictability, and robustness** in dynamic environments.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin (DT) system operates with a **real-time synchronization paradigm**, where data is continuously collected from the Physical Twin (PT) via sensors (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator case study) and fed into the DT framework. The temporal granularity depends on the **sampling rate of the sensors** and the **latency requirements of the calibration algorithms**, which update the models (e.g., differential equations for dynamic behavior) to reflect the PT's current state. While the document does not specify exact synchronization frequencies, the DT must support **low-latency data ingestion** to enable real-time monitoring (e.g., fault detection, state estimation) and **predictive simulations** under current operating conditions. The trade-off between model accuracy and computational speed (e.g., simulation runtime) is acknowledged, as highly accurate models may introduce delays in analysis. The DT’s temporal dynamics are further constrained by the **control loop** (e.g., Raspberry Pi-based temperature controller in the incubator), which requires timely data to adjust actuators (e.g., heatbed, fan) and maintain system stability. The overall time scale is thus **event-driven**, prioritizing responsiveness to PT state changes while balancing computational overhead for model updates and algorithm execution (e.g., optimizers, supervisory controllers).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin system supports **multiple instances of models** representing different aspects of the Physical Twin (PT) and its environment, structured hierarchically by abstraction levels and functional domains. In the incubator case study, **two distinct models** were explicitly built (Section 3), each calibrated to reflect specific behaviors (e.g., thermal dynamics, control logic). The architecture does not prescribe a centralized or decentralized deployment, but the schematic (Fig. 1) implies a **modular design** where:
  - **Cyber-Physical System (CPS) models** (e.g., differential equations for temperature regulation) are updated via **calibration algorithms** using real-time sensor data.
  - **Environmental models** (e.g., external temperature influences) are integrated to contextualize PT behavior.
  The scope of responsibilities is divided by **functional silos**: monitors (safety/optimality checks), calibration modules (parameter estimation), and decision-making entities (human/algorithm-driven interventions). While the document does not detail coordination mechanisms for multiple DT instances, the implication is that **loosely coupled components** (e.g., monitors, controllers) interact via shared data streams (sensor inputs, model outputs), with potential for **hierarchical coordination** if extended to larger systems (e.g., federated DTs).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **service-oriented architecture** centered on three core orchestration pillars: **data ingestion**, **model-driven simulation**, and **algorithm-based decision-making**, as depicted in Fig. 1. The constellation is characterized by:
  - **Component Integration**: Sensors (e.g., temperature probes) feed real-time data into the DT, which is processed by **calibration algorithms** to refine models of the CPS and environment. These models are then utilized by **monitors** (safety/performance validators) and **controllers** (e.g., supervisory logic for actuator adjustments).
  - **Resource Management**: Computational resources are allocated dynamically to balance **model fidelity** (e.g., high-accuracy differential equations) and **real-time responsiveness** (e.g., low-latency fault detection). The incubator case study exemplifies this via a **Raspberry Pi-based controller**, suggesting lightweight yet reactive orchestration.
  - **System-Wide Coordination**: The DT employs a **feedback loop** where deviations (e.g., temperature drift) trigger corrective actions (e.g., fan/heatbed adjustments). Human-in-the-loop interventions are possible for non-automated decisions, implying a **hybrid orchestration** of algorithmic and manual oversight. The architecture leverages **modularity** (e.g., separable models for cyber/physical domains) and **adaptive calibration** to maintain synchronization with the PT’s evolving state.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent and analyze the behavior of the Physical Twin (PT). Models include **abstract representations** of the PT's cyber and physical aspects, such as:
  - **Geometric/Structural Models**: CAD-based representations (e.g., car chassis shapes, incubator box dimensions) capturing physical attributes like sizes, shapes, and styles, but excluding dynamic interactions.
  - **Behavioral/Dynamic Models**: Mathematical formulations (e.g., differential equations) describing system dynamics, such as forces acting on a car or thermal behavior in an incubator. The incubator case study employed **two simulation models**—one with **two free parameters** and another with **four free parameters**—to predict internal temperature, with the latter demonstrating superior accuracy in modeling **thermal inertia** of the heatbed.
  - **Environmental Models**: Representations of external factors (e.g., wind, road surface for a car; ambient conditions for an incubator) that influence PT behavior.
  **Data** is sourced from **sensors** on the PT, collected over time, and used to:
  - Calibrate models via **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation for thermal inertia).
  - Update models dynamically to reflect the PT's current state (e.g., real-time adjustments to differential equations or simulation parameters).
  **Model relationships** are hierarchical: lower-fidelity models (e.g., 2-parameter incubator model) may serve as foundational inputs for higher-fidelity models (e.g., 4-parameter model). **Data management** involves integrating sensor streams with model outputs to enable **real-time monitoring** and **predictive simulations** (e.g., fault detection, state estimation). The DT framework leverages **algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, optimizers) to process data and models, generating actionable insights or triggering corrective actions (e.g., human/automated interventions when PT deviations are detected).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin relies on a **sensing infrastructure** to monitor the Physical Twin (PT) in real time. While specific sensor types, spatial distributions, or sampling frequencies are not detailed in the documents, the incubator case study implies:
  - **Temperature Sensors**: Deployed within the incubator to measure internal thermal conditions, feeding data to simulation models (e.g., 2-parameter or 4-parameter thermal models).
  - **Data Transmission**: Sensor data is transmitted to the cyber component (e.g., embedded computers or controllers) for processing, likely via **networked communication protocols** (e.g., Controller Area Network (CAN) in the car example or proprietary protocols in the incubator).
  - **Sampling and Accuracy**: The incubator’s thermal models require precise measurements to calibrate free parameters (e.g., thermal inertia), suggesting high-precision sensors with **consistent sampling rates** to ensure model fidelity. The documents do not specify exact frequencies, but the emphasis on model validation implies **high-resolution data collection** to distinguish between model variants (e.g., 2 vs. 4 parameters).
  **Data collection mechanisms** involve integrating sensor outputs with simulation models to update the DT’s representation of the PT dynamically. For example, the incubator’s DT uses sensor data to refine thermal predictions, enabling real-time adjustments to the heatbed’s control system.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin’s **fidelity and validity** are governed by rigorous **validation and verification (V&V) methods**, **uncertainty quantification**, and **quality assurance (QA)** processes. Key aspects include:
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements**: Higher-fidelity models (e.g., the incubator’s **4-parameter thermal model**) are preferred when they better capture critical PT behaviors (e.g., thermal inertia). Fidelity is assessed through **experimental validation**, where model predictions are compared against real-world PT performance (e.g., temperature measurements in the incubator).
  - **Validation Methods**: Empirical testing (e.g., running the incubator with known thermal inputs and comparing predicted vs. actual temperatures) to verify model accuracy. The incubator case study explicitly demonstrates that the **4-parameter model outperformed the 2-parameter model** in predicting thermal dynamics, justifying its use for further DT development.
  - **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**: Model parameters (e.g., free parameters in thermal equations) introduce uncertainty, which is mitigated through **calibration algorithms** that adjust parameters using real-time sensor data. The documents imply **statistical or optimization-based techniques** to quantify and reduce uncertainty (e.g., minimizing prediction errors).
  - **Error Detection and Correction**: The DT framework includes **monitors** that detect deviations between the PT’s actual state and model predictions. When violations (e.g., unsafe temperatures or dynamic behavior) are identified, **corrective actions** are triggered, such as:
    - **Automated adjustments** (e.g., recalibrating model parameters or altering control inputs).
    - **Human intervention** (e.g., engineers manually overriding the system or refining models).
  - **Quality Assurance Processes**: Continuous V&V loops ensure models remain aligned with the PT’s evolving state. For instance, the incubator’s open-source hardware/software platform enables **reproducible validation**, allowing developers to test and refine DT techniques systematically. The documents emphasize **iterative improvement**, where models are discarded or updated based on performance metrics (e.g., prediction accuracy).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system collects **real-time sensor data** from the Physical Twin (PT) via **two temperature sensors inside the insulated container** and **one temperature sensor outside the container**. The data types transmitted include **analog temperature readings** (likely in °C or °F) with unspecified precision but assumed to be high-resolution given the control requirements. The communication protocol is **not explicitly detailed**, though the system implies a **cyber-physical system (CPS) integration** where sensor data is likely transmitted over a **local network (e.g., Wi-Fi, Ethernet, or serial communication)** to the Raspberry Pi controller. The **transmission frequency** is not specified, but the context suggests **periodic polling** (e.g., every 1–10 seconds) to ensure real-time monitoring and calibration. **Data quality assurance** is implied through **calibration algorithms** (as referenced in Document 1, Fig. 1) that adjust DT models to align with PT observations. **Preprocessing steps** may include **filtering (e.g., moving averages, Kalman filtering)** to mitigate noise, though specifics are absent. **Data integration mechanisms** involve merging sensor inputs with **environmental models** (e.g., thermal dynamics of the styrofoam box) and **cyber-physical models** (e.g., heatbed behavior) to generate a unified digital representation for simulation and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) exerts **control commands** on the Physical Twin (PT) via the **Raspberry Pi controller**, which acts as the interface between the digital and physical layers. The **command types** include **temperature setpoints** and **actuation signals** (e.g., heatbed power modulation, fan speed adjustments). The **control message format** is unspecified but likely follows a **structured protocol** (e.g., JSON, MQTT, or proprietary CPS messages) to ensure compatibility with the embedded system. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not detailed, though the system implies **closed-loop control** where the DT continuously validates commands against **safety constraints** (e.g., max/min temperature thresholds). **Feedback systems** are critical: the PT’s sensor data (temperature readings) is looped back to the DT for **real-time correction** of control actions. **Conflict resolution** is handled implicitly via the **calibration algorithms** (Document 1, Fig. 1), which reconcile discrepancies between the DT’s predictive models and observed PT behavior. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred through **monitoring algorithms** that verify whether the PT’s state (e.g., temperature stability) aligns with the DT’s predictions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator relies on a **cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture** where the **Raspberry Pi** serves as the central node for data acquisition, processing, and control. The **communication protocols** are not explicitly defined, but the system likely employs **standard CPS protocols** such as **MQTT (for lightweight IoT messaging), HTTP/REST (for configuration), or serial protocols (e.g., UART, I2C)** for sensor-actuator communication. The **network topology** is **star-shaped**, with the Raspberry Pi as the hub connecting to sensors (temperature probes) and actuators (heatbed, fan). **Bandwidth requirements** are minimal, given the **low-data-rate sensor inputs** (temperature readings), but **latency must be sub-second** to ensure real-time control and safety. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not specified, though the use of **multiple temperature sensors** (two inside, one outside) suggests **sensor fusion** for fault tolerance. **Security protocols** are implied but not detailed; typical CPS security measures (e.g., **TLS for network traffic, authentication tokens for command validation**) may be applied, though the documents do not specify.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator is **hosted on a Raspberry Pi**, an **edge-computing device** co-located with the Physical Twin (PT) to minimize latency. The **computational requirements** are modest, involving **real-time sensor data processing, model calibration, and control algorithm execution**, which can be handled by the Pi’s **ARM-based processor** (e.g., quad-core 1.2–1.5 GHz). **Storage requirements** are minimal, primarily for **model parameters, calibration logs, and historical sensor data**, likely stored in **local flash memory or an SD card**. **Scalability mechanisms** are not addressed, as the system is designed for a **single, small-scale CPS**. The **deployment model** is **on-premise/edge**, with no mention of cloud integration. **Availability/reliability assurance** is implied through the **closed-loop control system**, where the DT continuously monitors the PT’s state and adjusts dynamically. However, no explicit redundancy (e.g., backup Pi or cloud failover) is described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as inferred from the provided documents, includes the following phases: **(1) Modeling Phase**: Abstract representations of the Physical Twin (PT) are created at varying levels of abstraction (e.g., CAD models for geometric properties, dynamic models for physics-based behavior via differential equations). These models may range from high-level conceptualizations (e.g., shapes, styles) to low-level, computationally intensive simulations (e.g., force dynamics). **(2) Integration Phase**: Models are integrated into the DT framework alongside real-time **data** (collected via sensors from the PT) and **algorithms** (e.g., calibration, fault detection, state estimation). **(3) Deployment Phase**: The DT is deployed to monitor and interact with the PT, leveraging **calibration algorithms** to update models dynamically with sensor data, ensuring alignment between the virtual and physical systems. **(4) Operational Phase**: The DT operates in real-time, using **monitors** to assess system performance (safety, optimality) and enabling **human/algorithm-driven interventions** (e.g., adjustments to the PT or DT models). **(5) Evolution Phase**: The DT undergoes continuous refinement through iterative updates to models and algorithms, driven by new data or environmental changes. **Transition Management**: The documents highlight the importance of retaining models post-deployment and integrating them into the DT, suggesting a seamless transition from design/analysis to operational use. **Lifecycle Governance**: While not explicitly detailed, the framework implies governance through **calibration-driven model updates** and **monitoring-based feedback loops**, ensuring adaptability and robustness. The Incubator case study exemplifies this lifecycle by building two models with calibration methods (Section 3) and validating them experimentally (Section 4).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The **twinning process** for the Digital Twin (DT) system, as described in the documents, follows a structured methodology centered on **modeling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization**, with a focus on the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** paradigm. **(1) Engineering Methodologies**: The process begins with **abstract modeling** of the PT (e.g., CAD for geometry, differential equations for dynamics) and progresses to **integrated simulation** using calibrated models. The Incubator case study employs **two distinct models** (Section 3), each incorporating **calibration techniques** to align virtual representations with real-world behavior. **(2) Quality Assurance**: The DT framework relies on **real-time sensor data** (e.g., temperature sensors in the Incubator) to validate model accuracy and system performance. **Calibration algorithms** ensure models remain synchronized with the PT’s current state, reducing discrepancies. **(3) Testing and Validation**: Experimental validation (Section 4) is conducted to verify DT predictions against PT behavior, with results used to refine models and algorithms. **(4) Version Control**: While not explicitly detailed, the iterative nature of calibration and model updates implies a **versioned evolution** of the DT, where older models may be retained for comparison or historical analysis. **(5) Evolution Management**: The DT evolves through **feedback-driven updates**, where deviations detected by monitors (e.g., safety violations) trigger adjustments to models or control algorithms. The Incubator’s **Raspberry Pi-based controller** exemplifies this by dynamically regulating temperature based on sensor feedback. **Technologies Used**: The process leverages **sensor networks** (e.g., temperature sensors), **control algorithms** (e.g., supervisory controllers), and **simulation tools** (e.g., differential equation solvers) to achieve twinning. The publicly available repository ([GitHub - INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) provides hardware/software specifications, suggesting a **modular, reproducible** approach to DT development.

extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study incorporates **safety-critical considerations** through its **monitoring and calibration mechanisms**, which ensure the system operates within predefined operational boundaries. Specifically, the DT employs **monitors** that check whether the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** and its environment are performing **safely and optimally** (as depicted in Figure 1). These monitors likely rely on **real-time data** from sensors (e.g., temperature sensors inside and outside the insulated container) to detect deviations or violations, triggering **corrective actions** via human intervention or automated algorithms. While the documents do not explicitly mention **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, access control, or authentication systems), the DT framework inherently requires **secure data transmission** between the Physical Twin (PT) and the DT to prevent unauthorized access or tampering. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implied through the calibration algorithms that update models to reflect the **best estimate of parameters**, ensuring the system remains robust against environmental or operational changes. However, no explicit details on **risk assessment approaches**, **data anonymization**, or **compliance with data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR) are provided in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the

**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.
